commit 295b711b5adcf70ae9e70a26c7fa99f9ce75d288
Author: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>
mm/vmscan: unify writeback reclaim statistic and throttling

This commit unifies the writeback reclaim statistic and throttling handling
between MGLRU and non-MGLRU by introducing a handle_reclaim_writeback()
helper. It aims to fix premature OOMs and LRU spinning caused by missing
congestion control under MGLRU.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -4833,26 +4836,11 @@ static int evict_folios(unsigned long nr_to_scan, struct lruvec *lruvec,
>  retry:
>  	reclaimed = shrink_folio_list(&list, pgdat, sc, &stat, false, memcg);
>  	sc->nr_reclaimed += reclaimed;
> +	handle_reclaim_writeback(isolated, pgdat, sc, &stat);

If MGLRU is performing global direct reclaim or kswapd reclaim, will the
statistics accumulated in sc->nr by handle_reclaim_writeback() be discarded
before they are evaluated?

In shrink_node(), when root_reclaim(sc) is true, the function returns early
after calling lru_gen_shrink_node():

	if ((lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_switching()) && root_reclaim(sc)) {
		...
		lru_gen_shrink_node(pgdat, sc);
		...
		return;
	}

Because of this early return, the code at the end of shrink_node() that sets
PGDAT_WRITEBACK and triggers reclaim_throttle() is bypassed. Without
PGDAT_WRITEBACK set, kswapd might not increment stat->nr_immediate for cyclic
writeback folios, preventing VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK from being invoked.

The cgroup reproducer in the commit message works because root_reclaim(sc)
is false during cgroup reclaim, allowing shrink_node() to reach the
throttling logic. Does this mean the regression of premature OOMs under
global memory pressure is still present for MGLRU?

>  	trace_mm_vmscan_lru_shrink_inactive(pgdat->node_id,
>  			type_scanned, reclaimed, &stat, sc->priority,
>  			type ? LRU_INACTIVE_FILE : LRU_INACTIVE_ANON);

[ ... ]

Target Commit:
commit 295b711b5adcf70ae9e70a26c7fa99f9ce75d288
Author: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>
Date:   Sat Mar 28 19:52:38 2026 +0000

    mm/vmscan: unify writeback reclaim statistic and throttling
    
    Currently MGLRU and non-MGLRU handle the reclaim statistic and
    writeback handling very differently, especially throttling.
    Basically MGLRU just ignored the throttling part.
    
    Let's just unify this part, use a helper to deduplicate the code
    so both setups will share the same behavior. Also remove the
    folio_clear_reclaim in isolate_folio which was actively invalidating
    the congestion control. PG_reclaim is now handled by shrink_folio_list,
    keeping it in isolate_folio is not helpful.
    
    Test using following reproducer using bash:
    
      echo "Setup a slow device using dm delay"
      dd if=/dev/zero of=/var/tmp/backing bs=1M count=2048
      LOOP=$(losetup --show -f /var/tmp/backing)
      mkfs.ext4 -q $LOOP
      echo "0 $(blockdev --getsz $LOOP) delay $LOOP 0 0 $LOOP 0 1000" | \
          dmsetup create slow_dev
      mkdir -p /mnt/slow && mount /dev/mapper/slow_dev /mnt/slow
    
      echo "Start writeback pressure"
      sync && echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
      mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/test_wb
      echo 128M > /sys/fs/cgroup/test_wb/memory.max
      (echo $BASHPID > /sys/fs/cgroup/test_wb/cgroup.procs && \
          dd if=/dev/zero of=/mnt/slow/testfile bs=1M count=192)
    
      echo "Clean up"
      echo "0 $(blockdev --getsz $LOOP) error" | dmsetup load slow_dev
      dmsetup resume slow_dev
      umount -l /mnt/slow && sync
      dmsetup remove slow_dev
    
    Before this commit, `dd` will get OOM killed immediately if
    MGLRU is enabled. Classic LRU is fine.
    
    After this commit, congestion control is now effective and no more
    spin on LRU or premature OOM.
    
    Stress test on other workloads also looking good.
    
    Suggested-by: Chen Ridong <chenridong@huaweicloud.com>
    Signed-off-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>

diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
index 1783da54ada1..83c8fdf8fdc4 100644
--- a/mm/vmscan.c
+++ b/mm/vmscan.c
@@ -1942,6 +1942,44 @@ static int current_may_throttle(void)
 	return !(current->flags & PF_LOCAL_THROTTLE);
 }
 
+static void handle_reclaim_writeback(unsigned long nr_taken,
+				     struct pglist_data *pgdat,
+				     struct scan_control *sc,
+				     struct reclaim_stat *stat)
+{
+	/*
+	 * If dirty folios are scanned that are not queued for IO, it
+	 * implies that flushers are not doing their job. This can
+	 * happen when memory pressure pushes dirty folios to the end of
+	 * the LRU before the dirty limits are breached and the dirty
+	 * data has expired. It can also happen when the proportion of
+	 * dirty folios grows not through writes but through memory
+	 * pressure reclaiming all the clean cache. And in some cases,
+	 * the flushers simply cannot keep up with the allocation
+	 * rate. Nudge the flusher threads in case they are asleep.
+	 */
+	if (stat->nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken && nr_taken) {
+		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
+		/*
+		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
+		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
+		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
+		 *
+		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
+		 * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
+		 * on a large system.
+		 */
+		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
+			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
+	}
+
+	sc->nr.dirty += stat->nr_dirty;
+	sc->nr.congested += stat->nr_congested;
+	sc->nr.writeback += stat->nr_writeback;
+	sc->nr.immediate += stat->nr_immediate;
+	sc->nr.taken += nr_taken;
+}
+
 /*
  * shrink_inactive_list() is a helper for shrink_node().  It returns the number
  * of reclaimed pages
@@ -2005,39 +2043,7 @@ static unsigned long shrink_inactive_list(unsigned long nr_to_scan,
 	lruvec_lock_irq(lruvec);
 	lru_note_cost_unlock_irq(lruvec, file, stat.nr_pageout,
 					nr_scanned - nr_reclaimed);
-
-	/*
-	 * If dirty folios are scanned that are not queued for IO, it
-	 * implies that flushers are not doing their job. This can
-	 * happen when memory pressure pushes dirty folios to the end of
-	 * the LRU before the dirty limits are breached and the dirty
-	 * data has expired. It can also happen when the proportion of
-	 * dirty folios grows not through writes but through memory
-	 * pressure reclaiming all the clean cache. And in some cases,
-	 * the flushers simply cannot keep up with the allocation
-	 * rate. Nudge the flusher threads in case they are asleep.
-	 */
-	if (stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken) {
-		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
-		/*
-		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
-		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
-		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
-		 *
-		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
-		 * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
-		 * on a large system.
-		 */
-		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
-			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
-	}
-

-	sc->nr.congested += stat.nr_congested;
-	sc->nr.writeback += stat.nr_writeback;
-	sc->nr.immediate += stat.nr_immediate;
-	sc->nr.taken += nr_taken;
-
+	handle_reclaim_writeback(nr_taken, pgdat, sc, &stat);
 	trace_mm_vmscan_lru_shrink_inactive(pgdat->node_id,
 			nr_scanned, nr_reclaimed, &stat, sc->priority, file);
 	return nr_reclaimed;
@@ -4651,9 +4657,6 @@ static bool isolate_folio(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct folio *folio, struct sca
 	if (!folio_test_referenced(folio))
 		set_mask_bits(&folio->flags.f, LRU_REFS_MASK, 0);
 
-	/* for shrink_folio_list() */
-	folio_clear_reclaim(folio);
-
 	success = lru_gen_del_folio(lruvec, folio, true);
 	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE_FOLIO(!success, folio);
 
@@ -4833,26 +4836,11 @@ static int evict_folios(unsigned long nr_to_scan, struct lruvec *lruvec,
 retry:
 	reclaimed = shrink_folio_list(&list, pgdat, sc, &stat, false, memcg);
 	sc->nr_reclaimed += reclaimed;
+	handle_reclaim_writeback(isolated, pgdat, sc, &stat);
 	trace_mm_vmscan_lru_shrink_inactive(pgdat->node_id,
 			type_scanned, reclaimed, &stat, sc->priority,
 			type ? LRU_INACTIVE_FILE : LRU_INACTIVE_ANON);

-		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

Okay, so this commit's about unifying how we handle writeback statistics and throttling between MGLRU and the non-MGLRU paths in `mm/vmscan.c`.  Initially, it looks like MGLRU was ignoring some of the throttling logic. The author's introduced a helper, `handle_reclaim_writeback`, to share this behavior and deduplicate the code. Initially, `evict_folios` isolates folios, and `isolated` will track this, but if `shrink_folio_list` is called in a retry loop the `isolated` value may not reflect this. The important part is how `handle_reclaim_writeback` uses `nr_taken`, which is compared to `stat->nr_unqueued_dirty`. If the number taken is the same as the number dirty we will wake up flushers and throttle, which is expected.

Before this commit, the check `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == isolated` was performed and flushers would be woken, and now it should be the same. The `handle_reclaim_writeback` function gets `nr_taken` and now that value is added to `sc->nr.taken` .  In a retry loop, `isolated` becomes 0, but the other `sc->nr` stats (`dirty`, `congested`, etc.) are *still* updated. The initial value of `nr_taken` can be compared to the dirty and used to throttle, but the fact that `isolated` can be zeroed in retries needs to be considered. Then I realized `shrink_folio_list` initializes the `stat` struct using `memset(stat, 0, sizeof(*stat))`. This means the `stat` will just include the counts from the *retried* folios, which seems reasonable.

In MGLRU `evict_folios`, the `sc->nr.taken += nr_taken;` is added inside `handle_reclaim_writeback`, which seems intentional, unifying the stats. Before this commit, MGLRU didn't update these stats, so it seems like the goal is to make it consistent with the classic LRU behavior. This means the MGLRU path is now included in the same heuristics used by `vmpressure` and `kswapd`.

Lastly, the commit removes `folio_clear_reclaim(folio)` from `isolate_folio`.  The rationale is that `PG_reclaim` is now handled by `shrink_folio_list`, and keeping it in `isolate_folio` is redundant.  Before this change, only `isolate_folio` (for MGLRU) called this function, while the classic LRU's `isolate_lru_folios` didn't.  `PG_reclaim` indicates a folio is marked for reclaim.  Now, the question is, does removing `folio_clear_reclaim` from the isolation path affect behavior? `shrink_folio_list` checks `folio_test_reclaim(folio)`. If it sees the bit set, it means that `shrink_folio_list` might handle things differently, which affects the congestion control!

The commit message's claim about invalidating the congestion control makes sense in this context.  If MGLRU was clearing `PG_reclaim` *before* `shrink_folio_list` got to it, `shrink_folio_list` would always operate as though the folio *wasn't* marked for reclaim. Now that we don't clear the reclaim bit, `shrink_folio_list` will see the bit set and adjust behavior accordingly.  For example, it affects writeback behavior if the `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` flag is also set.

Overall, it appears the commit is a well-considered effort to harmonize MGLRU's behavior with the rest of the VM subsystem. The added code clarity from the helpers, and aligning the statistics gathering are welcome. The removal of `folio_clear_reclaim` is a subtle but important correction to ensure congestion control is handled correctly. No apparent concerns.

Okay, so I'm diving into how `sc->nr.taken`, `sc->nr.writeback`, and other statistics are handled within the MGLRU reclaim retry loop. The goal is to ensure we're not double-counting folios, particularly those that might be re-processed in the retry phase.

My initial thought was, "Aha! `sc->nr.taken` tracks the total folios taken, period." And the code snippet in `shrink_node` seems to confirm this because it uses `sc->nr.taken` in its writeback check. That makes sense.

Now, let's trace the logic. `evict_folios` kicks off, potentially triggering a retry loop. On the first pass, we grab `isolated` folios using `handle_reclaim_writeback`. We update both `sc->nr.taken` (by adding `isolated`) and `sc->nr.writeback` (using `stat->nr_writeback`).  But the important thing to remember is the potential for retry, where `isolated` is 0 but `stat->nr_writeback` is updated based on the folios retried.

The retry loop is interesting. `shrink_folio_list` gets called again, but this time it's only processing the folios that were *specifically* filtered out in the first pass because they missed `folio_rotate_reclaimable`. The retry code explicitly checks:  `!folio_test_active`, `!folio_mapped`, `!folio_test_dirty`, and `!folio_test_writeback`. So, we *should* only be processing clean folios on the retry.

This raises the question:  Can a folio become writeback *between* the initial check and the retry pass? Technically, yes, though it's less likely. However, the critical point is that `stat->nr_writeback` should be zero during the retry since only clean folios are processed.

If `stat->nr_writeback` is zero, then `sc->nr.writeback` will be either unchanged, or correctly updated. If `stat->nr_writeback` is *non-zero* during a retry pass (which should not happen), then `sc->nr.writeback` would get inflated. Also `sc->nr.taken` would *not* be increased on retry because `isolated` is 0. This seems correct.

The challenge is the potential for double-counting. We counted `isolated` folios on the first pass, so if we re-counted them on the retry, we'd be off.  But, on the retry, we only touch folios that meet the criteria. That's good, because only a subset of the first-pass folios are re-processed.

So, on the first pass, `shrink_folio_list` processes *all* `isolated` folios and returns `stat->nr_writeback`, etc. We add them to `sc->nr.writeback`, etc. On retry, we call `shrink_folio_list` AGAIN. So, the question becomes: Will this `shrink_folio_list` call produce `stat` values that are non-zero for the same folios, or are the numbers correct?

If a folio *were* still writeback, and counted again, that would be double-counting. But, *those are filtered!*  Same for dirty folios: they are filtered as well.

What about other statistics? `stat->nr_congested` depends on writeback and reclaim, which, as these folios are clean, should be zero.  Same goes for `stat->nr_immediate` and `stat->nr_unqueued_dirty` – they require either writeback, or dirty, so those are zero on the retry.

So, if the retry only processes CLEAN folios, then `stat` fields should be zero. If we're adding zero to our counters on the retry, then we are safe from double-counting.

Wait a second. What about `sc->nr.dirty`?  Clean folios are NOT dirty, so `stat->nr_dirty` should *also* be zero.

The last thing to do is carefully review `shrink_folio_list` itself to confirm whether `stat` can return any non-zero values for *clean* folios (NOT mapped, active, writeback).

    "thought": "**Deep Dive into `evict_folios` Reclaim Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was about how `shrink_folio_list` interacts with the `stat` structure, particularly the `nr_demoted` field.  I was concerned about the potential for incorrect accounting because of the `retry` loop in `evict_folios`. The key point is that `shrink_folio_list` resets `stat` on each iteration of the loop, which led me to initially suspect that demotion counts might only reflect the *second* pass.  I needed to double-check where `mod_lruvec_state` which uses `stat.nr_demoted` was called.

But looking at the *original* code, it was called *outside* the loop! Okay, so I thought, the first pass is lost, but then the original code was correct! Wait - this must already be a bug, right?

I then carefully re-examined the code block surrounding the `retry` label. The crucial observation is that `mod_lruvec_state` and `handle_reclaim_writeback` are called *before* the `goto retry`.  So, the `stat` values are being processed from the *current* pass before the next iteration begins.

This means on the first pass, `handle_reclaim_writeback` *does* receive the first pass's `stat` values (for example `stat->nr_dirty`). On the second pass, `handle_reclaim_writeback` receives the second pass's `stat` values. This ensures that the state changes are correct. Additionally, `sc->nr_reclaimed` is incremented by the `reclaimed` value *from each pass*, which means that the total reclaimed value is correctly accounted for.

I then considered `trace_mm_vmscan_lru_shrink_inactive`, and its relationship with `type_scanned`. This is the variable tracking the number of folios scanned. I quickly realized this issue of double-counting scan values was already present in the original code, and is a pre-existing issue.

Finally, I checked `handle_reclaim_writeback`'s connection with `nr_taken` in the classic LRU reclaim. The important point is that, in the classic LRU reclaim case, `nr_taken` which indicates throttled state, is the value passed to `handle_reclaim_writeback`. However, in `evict_folios`, it's using the `isolated` value. What's the relationship between the two? I needed to confirm what `isolated` represents within the `evict_folios` context; whether it's the number of pages or folios. The context seems different between the two cases. Time to check `isolate_folios`.

The initial comparison of `stat->nr_unqueued_dirty` with `nr_taken` looks sound, as both are indeed in units of pages.

Now, the core concern: how are `sc->nr.taken`, `sc->nr.writeback`, and other `sc->nr` fields being updated in `evict_folios`? Before this patch, MGLRU effectively ignored these updates, leading to a situation where the throttle checks (based on these `sc->nr` fields) were bypassed. The patch introduces updates, adding `nr_taken` (which is the isolated pages on the first pass) to `sc->nr.taken` and updating all the stats fields.

The question then becomes: are these updates correct, especially during the retry pass? Specifically, on the retry pass, `handle_reclaim_writeback(0, pgdat, sc, &stat)` is called. I need to confirm that `stat` will have appropriate values. Given the filtering logic, the dirty/writeback folios are excluded from the retry list. Therefore, `stat->nr_dirty`, `stat->nr_writeback`, etc., will be zero on the retry pass. This ensures that the code doesn't double-count pages. I need to be sure!

Now, what about `stat->nr_congested` and `stat->nr_immediate`? These will also be zero for clean folios, ensuring no unintentional accumulation on the retry pass. So, `handle_reclaim_writeback(0, pgdat, sc, &stat)` acts as a no-op when processing the clean folios. This is safe.

Next question: is the removal of `folio_clear_reclaim(folio)` from `isolate_folio` problematic? Specifically, this removal means that folios that were writeback and were marked with `PG_reclaim` will keep this flag. `shrink_folio_list` checks `folio_test_reclaim(folio)` and potentially accumulates `stat->nr_congested` and sets `stat->nr_immediate`. Okay, so I'm diving into this code, and it's hitting me: the classic LRU's `isolate_lru_folios` doesn't clear `PG_reclaim`. That's standard. But, here's the rub – MGLRU *used* to clear `PG_reclaim` during isolation. Why? Digging into it, I see `folio_clear_reclaim(folio)` was explicitly in MGLRU's `isolate_folio`.

Then it hits me, the commit author removed it to unify MGLRU with classic LRU. They said "remove the folio_clear_reclaim in isolate_folio which was actively invalidating the congestion control. PG_reclaim is now handled by shrink_folio_list, keeping it in isolate_folio is not helpful." Makes sense at face value.

However, if `isolate_folio` *had* cleared `PG_reclaim` *before* `shrink_folio_list` was called, `shrink_folio_list` would *always* see `folio_test_reclaim(folio) == false`. And that, my friend, is a big problem. This means that `shrink_folio_list` would have *never* incremented `stat->nr_congested` or `stat->nr_immediate` for MGLRU. Those counters *require* `folio_test_reclaim(folio)` to be true!

Specifically, `if (writeback && folio_test_reclaim(folio)) stat->nr_congested += nr_pages;` This is where the magic happens! If `isolate_folio` cleared it, MGLRU would have *never* seen `PG_reclaim` as true inside `shrink_folio_list`. This completely broke MGLRU's congestion control! No wonder the commit author stated: "Basically MGLRU just ignored the throttling part. Let's just unify this part... Also remove the folio_clear_reclaim in isolate_folio which was actively invalidating the congestion control."

So, removing `folio_clear_reclaim` from `isolate_folio` is absolutely correct! It restores the ability of `shrink_folio_list` to see `PG_reclaim` and properly account for `nr_congested` and `nr_immediate`, just like classic LRU. The author really nailed it; they've made MGLRU *correct*.

This could lead to incorrect throttling decisions because `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` was computed only for the *remaining* folios on the list, which by definition, would be less than the original `isolated` value. Thus, the retry condition was virtually never met.

The fix introduces `isolated = 0;` before the `goto retry`. This ensures that on subsequent iterations the loop functions as intended, and `stat->nr_unqueued_dirty` is compared against zero. Crucially, the code still preserves the functionality of the old code by adding a check for `nr_taken`, and the combination of `stat->nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken && nr_taken` ensures a correct check. Also, the new code correctly updates `sc->nr.taken`. Previously, without resetting `isolated`, `sc->nr.taken` could be double-counted.

Another point to note is the update to `sc->nr.dirty`, etc. In the classic LRU, `sc->nr.dirty` was updated in `shrink_inactive_list`, which wasn't called in MGLRU. However, by adding `handle_reclaim_writeback()`, MGLRU *now* correctly tracks and accumulates these crucial metrics (`sc->nr.dirty`, etc.), which is critical for congestion throttling. The original code skipped this vital step, which is an oversight and a cause of incorrect throttling behavior.

Let's look at `sc->nr_scanned`. The classic LRU code was explicit about updating this in both `isolate_lru_folios` and `shrink_folio_list`. The key thing to remember here is that `isolate_lru_folios` returns `nr_scanned` and then adds this value to the state, while the call to `shrink_folio_list` also adds to `sc->nr_scanned`. I see that `isolate_lru_folios` actually returns a *copy* of scanned pages via the return value, while the internal value for `sc->nr_scanned` is only incremented inside `shrink_folio_list`. This implies that `sc->nr_scanned` ONLY counts folios that were *isolated*. If the MGLRU loop retries, it *does* double-count `sc->nr_scanned`. This is pre-existing behavior, though.

Now, concerning the handling of `stat->nr_demoted`. The code is structured to ensure that `mod_lruvec_state` which updates the internal stats is executed *at the end of the loop*. Those updates were exclusively within `shrink_inactive_list`, which is for the classic LRU algorithm.

With `isolated` being 0, `nr_taken` will also be 0 in the call to `handle_reclaim_writeback`.

Here's the critical point: in `handle_reclaim_writeback`, we have `sc->nr.dirty += stat->nr_dirty;` etc. But now `isolated` is 0. Now, let's move onto `evict_folios`, which is the MGLRU path. Here, `handle_reclaim_writeback` is called *after* `shrink_folio_list`, *after* the `lruvec` lock has been released. Okay, so I'm digging into how dirty folio counts are handled during writeback and reclaim, specifically looking at potential inaccuracies. Hmm, interesting. It seems that `evict_folios` repeatedly sets `isolated = 0; goto retry;` if the list of folios isn't empty after a pass. Each time through this loop, `shrink_folio_list` gets called, and it processes the remaining folios, which updates a `stat` structure. Then, `handle_reclaim_writeback` grabs `stat.nr_dirty` etc. and adds them to `sc->nr.dirty`.

Here's where it's potentially problematic. If a folio is skipped within `shrink_folio_list` during a first pass and remains in the `list`, its dirty state was counted in `stat.nr_dirty` during that *first* pass. But, we go to `goto retry`. Now, in the *second* pass of the loop, that same folio is counted in `stat.nr_dirty` *again*! Is this the behavior I think it is? Is this double-counting of a single folio?

Aha. If a folio is put back onto the `list` inside `shrink_folio_list`, that means it wasn't reclaimed, right? And that means it was probably dirty? Yes, maybe. So, that means `stat.nr_dirty` could be incremented every time `shrink_folio_list` examines the same folio! This is a possible flaw in the approach.

Okay, let's step back a bit and look at the bigger picture. Where is this `sc->nr.dirty` value actually used? Right, it's used to update the `reclaim_state` or `sc` fields, which are used for throttling or general statistics. The problem, as I see it, is that MGLRU's `evict_folios` function might be double-counting due to its retry mechanism. Since we accumulate `stat` from the same folios in retry passes, this may break the check `sc->nr.dirty == sc->nr.congested`.

My initial thought was that as long as *both* are incremented proportionally, the equality would be maintained.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `PG_reclaim` Flag and its Impact on MGLRU**

Okay, so I'm digging into this code and trying to understand the interaction between `PG_reclaim` and MGLRU. It's becoming clear that `PG_reclaim` is used to flag folios that are either immediately targeted for reclaim or were part of a readahead operation. Specifically, it seems that if `PG_reclaim` is set on a folio, it signifies that it's flagged for immediate reclamation.

I was looking at `shrink_folio_list`. I see that within that function, there's a conditional check:

```c
		if (writeback && folio_test_reclaim(folio))
			stat->nr_congested += nr_pages;
...
		if (folio_test_writeback(folio)) {
...
			if (current_is_kswapd() &&
			    folio_test_reclaim(folio) &&
			    test_bit(PGDAT_WRITEBACK, &pgdat->flags)) {
				stat->nr_immediate += nr_pages;
				goto activate_locked;
```

It seems that the original code, *before the patch*, had an issue. In `isolate_folio`, which is part of MGLRU, `folio_clear_reclaim(folio)` was called unconditionally. This means that any folio moved by MGLRU would have its `PG_reclaim` flag cleared immediately. Because of this, `shrink_folio_list` would *never* register `folio_test_reclaim(folio)` as true for folios managed by MGLRU. So it wouldn't increment `stat->nr_congested` or `stat->nr_immediate` at all! This is a *big* issue.

The commit message acknowledges this, stating the problem and then resolving the issue by removing the `folio_clear_reclaim` call in `isolate_folio`. The commit makes a point of explaining that clearing the `PG_reclaim` flag in `isolate_folio` was counterproductive, since `shrink_folio_list` is the place that handles the flag.

Now, I need to make sure I understand this. The commit *removes* the `folio_clear_reclaim` from `isolate_folio` so that `shrink_folio_list` can work as expected.

But then I had a question, when and where *does* `shrink_folio_list` clear `PG_reclaim`?

```c
	if (folio_test_reclaim(folio) &&
	    (folio_test_dirty(folio) || folio_test_writeback(folio))) {
		/* ... */
	}
```

I need to confirm whether `shrink_folio_list` itself clears the flag under certain conditions. I need to track down the precise moment that it clears `PG_reclaim`. This seems important to understanding the entire flow.

Okay, let's break this down. This commit is removing `folio_clear_reclaim` from `isolate_folio`, which initially made me think about whether `isolate_folio` is called under `lruvec_lock_irq`. The commit message's goal is achieved.

I then started to scrutinize `shrink_folio_list`. Does this function actually clear the `PG_reclaim` flag for *all* folios? Nope, it doesn't do it universally, only as part of folio freeing. If a folio is reclaimed (freed), then the flag is cleared. If not reclaimed, then `PG_reclaim` *stays* set. If it's writeback and kept, `folio_end_writeback` then clears it. The crucial point here is `folio_end_writeback` will properly call `folio_clear_reclaim` when the writeback completes. So, leaving the flag set in `isolate_folio` is actually the *correct* behavior, resolving the bug.

Now, I shifted focus to the `retry` loop within `evict_folios`.  Specifically, how do the folios that "miss" `folio_rotate_reclaimable()` behave? Those folios are put back into the `clean` list. They were initially clean, then were writeback, and then they finished writeback. They were not active, mapped, dirty or writeback.

This retry loop is about handling folios that finished writeback *after* `shrink_folio_list` had already decided to put them on the LRU but *before* they were actually added to the LRU in `move_folios_to_lru()`. They were writeback in the *first* pass but finished writeback in the short window before the folios were actually moved to the LRU. This means they are on a *retry* after finishing writeback, missing `folio_rotate_reclaimable()`.

During the second pass (retry), these now clean folios are reclaimed. The critical point is that these folios *won't* have their writeback statistics double-counted! In the first pass, the `stat` variable accumulated data that's relevant to a writeback folio. On the second, they're clean, and `shrink_folio_list` will reclaim these folios, and `stat` will have already been cleared.

Crucially, the author added `isolated = 0;` within the `if (!list_empty(&list))` block and before the `goto retry;` command. Okay, so I'm diving deep into how MGLRU interacts with the reclaim process, specifically focusing on potential race conditions and lock ordering issues when throttling and handling isolated folios. I'm starting by tracing the execution path and trying to understand how different components interact.

I'm also confirming that MGLRU bypasses the `too_many_isolated` check, which seems fine because it's handling throttling differently.

Now, I'm focusing on the interaction between `evict_folios` in MGLRU and the potential for sleeping in `reclaim_throttle`. In Classic LRU, the sleep happens *after* moving folios back to the LRU, while in MGLRU, it happens *before*. The key question is whether this difference matters. If it sleeps *before* re-inserting, those folios are hidden from the rest of the system during the sleep, potentially causing problems if another process needs them. This means any other reclaimer cannot find them.

I'm thinking: is holding them on a local list during the sleep a serious issue? I need to consider potential deadlocks or incorrect refcounting. I'm rationalizing that `reclaim_throttle` is designed to be a bounded sleep to alleviate IO congestion, which is fine.

I'm also considering the `retry` loop. The key is that `sc->nr_reclaimed` is incremented. I'm checking if the `shrink_folio_list` is called again in the retry loop. If it is, is `reclaimed` from the second pass added to `sc->nr_reclaimed`? I see that it is!

First, the crucial bit: `lru_note_cost_unlock_irq` properly releases the `lruvec` lock and interrupts are re-enabled before `handle_reclaim_writeback` is called within `shrink_inactive_list`. And, `evict_folios` also explicitly unlocks the `lruvec` and disables interrupts. This means any blocking operations are safe. So, we are good on the locking front.

Now, my focus shifts to the tracepoints and potential issues. Specifically, I'm concerned about the `trace_mm_vmscan_lru_shrink_inactive` tracepoint and its interaction with the `retry` logic in `evict_folios`. This is where it gets interesting!

I noticed that `trace_mm_vmscan_lru_shrink_inactive` uses `&stat` in `evict_folios`.  The original code has a `retry` loop where the tracepoint was *already* inside.

The problem comes down to the values traced. The first pass through the loop, we have `type_scanned` which is the number of folios originally taken. Then, on the second `retry` pass, `type_scanned` is *not* updated, it's still the original count. So, in the trace event, `type_scanned` is double-reported. This could lead to inflated scan counts in any system that consumes this trace event!

Initially, I was worried this was a bug introduced by the patch, but upon further inspection, I see that the tracepoint was *already* in the `retry` loop. The patch only added `handle_reclaim_writeback` before it. So the trace issue is preexisting. I verified that by looking at the patch again.

I then honed in on the change: `isolated = 0;` added *before* `goto retry;`.  Why? `handle_reclaim_writeback` takes the `isolated` value and uses it as `nr_taken`. Before the patch, `isolated` was not zeroed on retry, which means there was potentially double-counting the "taken" folios. This new `isolated = 0;` prevents that double-counting, ensuring `sc->nr.taken` is incremented correctly only with *new* folios. The patch author was clearly thinking about the ramifications of the retry loop.

Finally, I checked the impact of removing `folio_clear_reclaim` from `isolate_folio`, specifically in the context of the MGLRU code.  By removing `folio_clear_reclaim(folio)`, `PG_reclaim` remains set. This change ensures proper congestion tracking within the MGLRU context by unifying the logic with Classic LRU behavior. Classic LRU's `isolate_lru_folios` function does not clear `PG_reclaim`, so by changing the MGLRU code to match, congestion tracking is consistent. That's good.

So, in conclusion, the patch is solid. It fixes a double-counting issue and aligns the congestion tracking in MGLRU with the established practices in Classic LRU. The tracepoint issue is a pre-existing problem that probably needs a separate fix, but this patch doesn't make it worse.

Okay, let's break this patch down and give it a thorough going-over. First, I'm noting that the original MGLRU implementation was an anomaly, specifically with its call to `folio_clear_reclaim(folio)` within `isolate_folio`. Classic LRU correctly uses `__folio_clear_lru` or `folio_test_clear_lru` and *doesn't* call `folio_clear_reclaim`. Removing this inconsistency is good because it was breaking `PG_reclaim`-based tracking for MGLRU.

Now, looking at the high-level changes: The code now uses `handle_reclaim_writeback` in `evict_folios` and `shrink_inactive_list`, replacing hardcoded blocks. A nice cleanup. In `evict_folios` the `isolated` variable is set to 0 before the `goto retry` which prevents double-counting, a good measure.

The new `handle_reclaim_writeback` helper function extracts the writeback flusher logic and importantly, consolidates the accumulation of reclaim statistics (`sc->nr.dirty`, `sc->nr.congested`, etc.). This is *new* behavior for MGLRU, unifying it with Classic LRU, which already does this. This looks right.

My initial concern was locking. `handle_reclaim_writeback` *itself* doesn't take locks, which is good. However, it *might* sleep inside `reclaim_throttle` if writeback throttling isn't sane (e.g., cgroupv2 writeback not being used). That's something to watch. This helper is called without the `lruvec` lock in both `shrink_inactive_list` and `evict_folios`. In `shrink_inactive_list`, folios are already back on the LRU before this helper is called, so potential sleeps are less of an issue. In `evict_folios`, however, it is called *before* folios are re-added to the LRU, and are either in `&list` or `&clean` local lists. A sleep here temporarily hides the folios from the rest of the system. However, `reclaim_throttle` sleeps for up to `HZ/10` (100ms), or until congestion is cleared. This is probably acceptable. In MGLRU, `too_many_isolated` isn't used, so isolation stalling isn't a concern. The local variables such as the `stat` struct are initialized within `shrink_folio_list`. The `nr_taken` logic in the retry path looks correct.

No RCU issues that I see – no `call_rcu`, `synchronize_rcu`, or `kfree_rcu` introduced. Pointers (`sc`, `pgdat`, `stat`) are all valid. `stat` is on the stack and passed by reference. `sc->nr` fields are initialized during `scan_control` initialization. No changes to the locking paths have been made.

I'm double-checking the `evict_folios` retry path. The concern was around `sc->nr_reclaimed` potentially double-counting. If we `goto retry`, those folios were *not* reclaimed on the first pass. If they *are* reclaimed during retry, `reclaimed` will be > 0 and the correct amount gets added to `sc->nr_reclaimed`. This appears correct.

The `handle_reclaim_writeback` logic specifically about `stat->nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken && nr_taken` looks sound. If `nr_taken` is zero (on the retry pass) the logic will short-circuit. This prevents throttling on retry which is as expected. If all folios are unqueued dirty, the logic is true. This matches the existing logic.

I think the `sc->nr.taken` accumulation is correct. The number of isolated folios returned from `isolate_folios` is added to `sc->nr.taken` in `evict_folios`, which is the same as what happens in `shrink_inactive_list`.

So overall, I'd say this patch is solid. It correctly handles the stats, prevents the double-counting issue on the retry pass, and fixes the `PG_reclaim` issue in MGLRU. The locking semantics are the same in both code paths. There aren't any obvious locking, RCU, memory management, or logical issues here.

Now, looking at how `handle_reclaim_writeback()` is used in `evict_folios`, that's where things get a bit more involved. `evict_folios` has a retry loop.     "thought": "**Analyzing the MGLRU Reclaim Flow**

Alright, let's break this down from the perspective of an expert familiar with the memory management intricacies. My initial observation is that `lru_gen_shrink_node(pgdat, sc)` sets the stage by configuring the plug and `mm_walk`, and then calls either `shrink_many(pgdat, sc)` or `shrink_one(&pgdat->__lruvec, sc)`.  `shrink_many` is particularly interesting because it iterates through memory control groups (memcgs) and invokes `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, which eventually calls `evict_folios()`.

Now, the core of the patch lies within `evict_folios()`. Specifically, the new `handle_reclaim_writeback()` function is crucial, where we see `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` is compared with `nr_taken`, and if they match and `nr_taken` is non-zero, it triggers `wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN)` and possibly `reclaim_throttle()`. Importantly, `handle_reclaim_writeback()` also accumulates stats into `sc->nr.dirty`, `sc->nr.congested`, `sc->nr.writeback`, `sc->nr.immediate`, and `sc->nr.taken`.

However, here's where my focus sharpens. I'm starting to question if the accumulated values in `sc->nr.writeback`, `sc->nr.immediate`, `sc->nr.dirty`, and `sc->nr.congested` are actually used effectively within the MGLRU context *after* the accumulation takes place. With MGLRU's global reclaim mechanism, `lru_gen_shrink_node()` seems to return directly. There's no equivalent check like the one at the end of `shrink_node()` that inspects `sc->nr.writeback` and the other counters. Specifically, there doesn't seem to be a check that's present in the standard `shrink_node()` function: `if (current_is_kswapd()) { if (sc->nr.writeback && sc->nr.writeback == sc->nr.taken) set_bit(PGDAT_WRITEBACK, &pgdat->flags); ... }`

So, the question is, *where* in `lru_gen_shrink_node()` or subsequent MGLRU-specific code are these accumulated values actually evaluated and used for decision-making? Is there an oversight here? It's critical to determine if this accumulation is merely informational, or if these values are intended for further logic that may be missing in the MGLRU code path. We need to follow the execution flow to see if the counters are ever checked to determine if we should set the `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` flag, or if they are just being accumulated for no apparent reason.

    "thought": "**MGLRU and Reclamation: A Throttling Conundrum**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial observation is that `shrink_one()` and `shrink_many()` seem to bypass the usual `sc->nr.*` counters, unless they indirectly touch them through `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. Then, `evict_folios()` calls `handle_reclaim_writeback()` which is where the aggregation of `sc->nr.*` occurs. The situation changes significantly with MGLRU and global reclaim. If `root_reclaim(sc)` is true, MGLRU invokes `shrink_node()`, calls `lru_gen_shrink_node()`, *and then immediately returns*!

The subsequent code, the logic which looks at `sc->nr.writeback` to set `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` and perform throttling in `kswapd`, is skipped entirely. This is concerning. It's supposed to assess `sc->nr.writeback` against `sc->nr.taken` and potentially set `PGDAT_WRITEBACK`. It also checks `sc->nr.immediate` for throttling, and sets congested flags for cgroup and node. This whole block, crucial for throttling and writeback management, is executed AFTER the MGLRU `return`.

So, in the MGLRU case with global reclaim, the counters, specifically `sc->nr.writeback`, are incremented during `evict_folios`, but the corresponding actions—setting `PGDAT_WRITEBACK`, congestion flags and applying `reclaim_throttle()`—are never performed!

This begs the question: *does MGLRU implement its own throttling mechanisms?* If so, where are they? I need to investigate if MGLRU has any independent throttling for `kswapd` or direct reclaim. This is a critical point that needs clarification.

    "thought": "**MGLRU and the Writeback Throttling Mystery**

Okay, let's break this down. In `mm/vmscan.c`, specifically around line 1185, I'm noticing something that's not quite right. The code has this check within `shrink_folio_list()`:

```c
			if (current_is_kswapd() &&
			    folio_test_reclaim(folio) &&
			    test_bit(PGDAT_WRITEBACK, &pgdat->flags)) {
				stat->nr_immediate += nr_pages;
				goto activate_locked;
```

For this to trigger under MGLRU when `current_is_kswapd()`, `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` needs to be set. The issue is, `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` is set *only* within `shrink_node()`. And from what I understand, this setting hinges on *either* `!root_reclaim(sc)` *or* `lru_gen_enabled()` being false. Hold on... after further digging, I can see that `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` is actually set *after* `shrink_node_memcgs()`. But with MGLRU and `root_reclaim(sc)` being true, `shrink_node()` returns early! That means `PGDAT_WRITEBACK` is *never* being set.

So, in the MGLRU scenario under global pressure, `stat->nr_immediate` is never incremented. Consequently, `sc->nr.immediate` remains at 0. This is a problem because the writeback throttling, driven by `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`, relies on `sc->nr.immediate`. Since it's zero, the throttling is effectively bypassed, and that's *because* of the early return!

The reviewer's analysis appears completely sound:

1.  `shrink_node()` indeed early exits for MGLRU when `root_reclaim(sc)` is true.
2.  The patch introduced `handle_reclaim_writeback(..., &stat)` within `evict_folios()`, and this *does* accumulate data into `sc->nr.*`.
3.  The early return in `shrink_node()` completely skips the logic that *actually* evaluates `sc->nr.*`, sets `PGDAT_WRITEBACK`, or calls `reclaim_throttle(..., VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`.
4.  Therefore, MGLRU under global reclaim still ignores the writeback congestion control and it looks as though the unification of logic is incomplete.

I should double check if MGLRU checks `sc->nr.writeback` anywhere else.
